title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a woman called Grace talking about volunteering at an animal rescue centre. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a woman called Grace talking about volunteering at an animal rescue centre. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A woman called Grace introduces volunteering opportunities at an animal rescue centre.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. My name’s Grace and I work at Meadow Farm Animal Rescue Centre. Despite its name, the centre is not on a working farm. The buildings were once used by a local [9]riding school[/9], so they include stables, storage rooms and fields which are ideal for animals needing space. When the school moved elsewhere, a charity took over the site and began caring for animals that had been abandoned or whose owners could no longer keep them. Today we look after cats, dogs, rabbits and a few larger animals.||Volunteers often apply because they would like to walk dogs, and this is certainly one useful role. However, the animals for which we currently need most daily assistance are our [10]rabbits[/10], as their living areas must be cleaned frequently and their food carefully prepared. New volunteers must be at least sixteen unless accompanying a parent. Before beginning practical tasks, everyone attends a short session about [11]animal behaviour[/11], helping them understand signs that an animal is nervous or does not want to be touched. This protects both people and animals.||The first task given to new helpers is not feeding animals, because different individuals have different diets. Instead, newcomers generally prepare clean [12]bedding[/12], putting the right material in cages and sleeping areas under supervision. We provide protective aprons and all necessary cleaning equipment. You should arrive in old clothes and bring [13]strong gloves[/13], particularly in winter when work outdoors can be cold and wet. The centre has a small café, so volunteers can buy hot drinks, although most people bring lunch from home.||Animals usually arrive after a phone call from a member of the public or a local vet. Each new arrival must be checked and given a quiet place to settle. Last month we completed a new [14]recovery room[/14], where animals can rest after treatment away from noise. Volunteers who prefer office work are also needed. They help update the centre's [15]website[/15] with photographs and descriptions of animals ready to be offered new homes. Accurate information is important because families often make their first enquiries after seeing an animal online.||We run several events to find new supporters. Last year's session on caring for older dogs was popular, but this year our first fully booked class is on [16]cat first aid[/16], led by one of our vets. People who volunteer every week for three months receive a [17]training certificate[/17], showing what responsibilities they have safely undertaken. Our greatest expense this year is improving outdoor areas. Instead of holding our usual sponsored run, we will raise funds through an [18]autumn fair[/18], including stalls, games and demonstrations by dog trainers. All money collected will pay for safer fences and covered spaces. Please take an information leaflet if you would like to help.

dialogue.speaker: grace
dialogue.text: Hello. My name’s Grace and I work at Meadow Farm Animal Rescue Centre. Despite its name, the centre is not on a working farm. The buildings were once used by a local riding school, so they include stables, storage rooms and fields which are ideal for animals needing space. When the school moved elsewhere, a charity took over the site and began caring for animals that had been abandoned or whose owners could no longer keep them. Today we look after cats, dogs, rabbits and a few larger animals.||Volunteers often apply because they would like to walk dogs, and this is certainly one useful role. However, the animals for which we currently need most daily assistance are our rabbits, as their living areas must be cleaned frequently and their food carefully prepared. New volunteers must be at least sixteen unless accompanying a parent. Before beginning practical tasks, everyone attends a short session about animal behaviour, helping them understand signs that an animal is nervous or does not want to be touched. This protects both people and animals.||The first task given to new helpers is not feeding animals, because different individuals have different diets. Instead, newcomers generally prepare clean bedding, putting the right material in cages and sleeping areas under supervision. We provide protective aprons and all necessary cleaning equipment. You should arrive in old clothes and bring strong gloves, particularly in winter when work outdoors can be cold and wet. The centre has a small café, so volunteers can buy hot drinks, although most people bring lunch from home.||Animals usually arrive after a phone call from a member of the public or a local vet. Each new arrival must be checked and given a quiet place to settle. Last month we completed a new recovery room, where animals can rest after treatment away from noise. Volunteers who prefer office work are also needed. They help update the centre's website with photographs and descriptions of animals ready to be offered new homes. Accurate information is important because families often make their first enquiries after seeing an animal online.||We run several events to find new supporters. Last year's session on caring for older dogs was popular, but this year our first fully booked class is on cat first aid, led by one of our vets. People who volunteer every week for three months receive a training certificate, showing what responsibilities they have safely undertaken. Our greatest expense this year is improving outdoor areas. Instead of holding our usual sponsored run, we will raise funds through an autumn fair, including stalls, games and demonstrations by dog trainers. All money collected will pay for safer fences and covered spaces. Please take an information leaflet if you would like to help.

Animals can rest after treatment in the new (14) .

questions.explanation: Grace says helpers update the centre's 'website'.
